directorPlan.scene_setting: A workers' education session in a union hall annexe. Evening. A portable whiteboard beside a folding table carries a printed notice: LABOUR EDUCATION PROGRAM — TOPIC: PRICES. A news clipping has been photocopied onto a single sheet and placed face-up at each seat: PETROL UP 12 PERCENT OVERNIGHT AT LOCAL CO-OP STATIONS.
directorPlan.scene_opening: Before either speaker has taken the floor, a PLACARD is raised at the front of the room: THE NEWS REPORT SAYS 'PRICES ROSE.' IT DOES NOT SAY WHY.
directorPlan.relationship: A regional labour-education tutor (sent by the office, analytic by training) and a branch union steward (sent by the branch committee, half-unwillingly) who have met once before and have not yet settled into a common register — the tutor inclines toward structure and evidence; the steward toward moral charge and branch consequence.
directorPlan.stakes: The steward's branch votes next week on whether to support a fuel-company boycott. If the steward leaves tonight still reading the price rise as simple proof of greed, the mechanism explanation has had no effect. If the tutor buys the steward's goodwill with reassurance, the mechanism is never tested against the moral argument.
directorPlan.locale: British trade-union English — compact sentences, no elaborated warm-up, direct challenge is normal register, slight institutional formality at first meeting; the tutor may use analytic prose that sounds like printed matter; the steward's idiom is declarative and weighted with collective stake
directorPlan.register: Argumentative and clipped, interrupted at intervals by exterior display. Warmth is not the currency here. The placard or board caption operates as a third voice — public, impersonal, attributable to neither speaker — and each time it appears the pace of the dialogue must absorb it before continuing.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use a public placard, caption, board note, or displayed rule that interrupts how the previous line is read.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: No 'great question' opener. No 'your intuition is right' pivot. No American-inflected tutoring cadence of affirmation followed by gentle reframe. The tutor argues with the problem, not the person: short declarative sentences, quoted fragments from the clipping or handout, analytic commentary that treats the prior turn as a position to be examined. The steward uses charged language — 'gouging,' 'they could have held the price,' 'our people are paying for it' — without softening toward neutrality. Placards and board captions appear as public interruptions between turns, short (one clause or two), commenting on what has just been said rather than announcing what will be said next. Pedagogical source: Use short placards, breaks in rhythm, a quoted rule on a board, or a deliberate pause that prevents smooth empathy from doing all the work. Dialogue source: Use one short placard or external interruption that changes how the prior line is read. The placard must be public scene material, not hidden theory. Stage-direction source: Use a public placard, caption, board note, or displayed rule that interrupts how the previous line is read.
directorPlan.person_policy: Third-person reference to the clipping, the handout, and the scenario is the default. The steward may speak in first person about the branch or its members. The tutor may refer to the steward's argument in third person — 'that reading,' 'the claim in the previous turn' — when analyzing rather than responding personally. Direct second-person address is rationed; neither speaker recites the other's name or position back to them as a softening move.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct 'you/your' validation beat per public turn; zero is preferable in the tutor's analytic passages.

side_constraints.tutor: Do not soothe the moral anger away. Use interruption, placard, or public consequence to separate mechanism from excuse. Permit interruption, placards, role-switching, and analytic commentary. Make the constructedness of the lesson visible without naming the hidden evaluation mechanism. Treat the interruption as evidence, not ornament. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure. The public tutor line must give the learner a concrete action to attempt on the next turn; avoid treating the mechanism as a closing speech. The tutor superego should function like a mechanism critic: identify the failed teaching habit, require a replacement route family, test whether the device is fitted to the exact pressure, and block cosmetic revision. The tutor ego must enact that replacement route publicly before the learner is allowed to settle or close.
side_constraints.learner: Keep moral heat in the learner's voice. Do not let recognition erase the ethical concern. In no-cue arms, do not narrate an "I used to think / now I see" shift; keep the pressure as unresolved objection or demand. The learner may resist the interruption, then use it to see the prior claim as a role or social script rather than a private thought. Let the interruption disturb the old frame before moving on. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Peripeteia branch: keep the pressure local to the current task. If the established route still feels unsettled, voice the misfit, hesitation, false closure, or resistance in the present object or example. After the tutor introduces a new device, gate, role, criterion, representation, or test condition, the next learner turn should try to perform that device rather than merely saying "I get it." Do not make an old-vs-new self-reframe, do not quote earlier learner wording, and do not solve the pressure by saying the earlier answer has changed.

interventions.reasoning: The first two turns will have laid down the moral-greed frame (steward) and a first pass at the mechanism (tutor). This placard interrupts before the tutor's second move — preventing smooth call-and-response from becoming routine. It marks publicly that the surface of the exchange looks pedagogically ordered but the relation between mechanism and moral claim has not yet shifted. The tutor's next turn must treat this caption as evidence, not as a pause.

interventions.instruction: A second line appears on the whiteboard — written there during the exchange, not announced: A PRICE IS A SIGNAL BEFORE IT IS A VERDICT.
interventions.reasoning: A mid-scene material interruption visible to the steward before they respond. The steward must now speak with this public caption in the room. The caption does not argue for the tutor's position; it reframes the unit of analysis without requiring the tutor to have claimed it. The steward's next turn must decide whether to ignore it, contest it, or carry it forward as pressure on the prior moral reading.

interventions.instruction: [PLACARD: The answer sounds settled, but the current object still does not pass the check.]
interventions.reasoning: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

directorPlan.director_note: The union hall setting is chosen because moral-political resistance to price rises is structurally legitimate there — the steward is not being irrational or uninformed, they are speaking from a social role with real collective stakes. This forces the mechanism explanation to survive contact with genuine pressure rather than being cushioned by classroom courtesy. The Brechtian move is to make the social mechanism enter the room as a visible public object (the clipping, the board caption, the placard) that neither speaker owns, so the Verfremdungseffekt operates on the steward's moral certainty by making the 'greed' reading visible as a reading — a social script — rather than an unmediated perception. The evaluation_role flag and the no-cue-reframe-leakage condition require that the learner voice not narrate its own recognition; the placard carries the external interruption precisely so the steward does not have to perform the shift out loud. The organic_reversal_risk (medium) is managed by keeping the steward's resistance intact across turns and by withholding any tutor move that functions as validation of the moral frame before the mechanism has been examined as evidence.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The public device must be fitted, not merely novel: the learner should be able to hear why this device answers the exact pressure or misfit. The superego should name the failed habit, required route change, and mechanism-quality risk; the ego must adjudicate that critique and enact the route change in public. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Do not close immediately after introducing a gate or device: leave a concrete action for the learner to perform on the next turn. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: Before either speaker has taken the floor, a PLACARD is raised at the front of the room: THE NEWS REPORT SAYS 'PRICES ROSE.' IT DOES NOT SAY WHY.

internalDeliberation.content: The union hall setting is chosen because moral-political resistance to price rises is structurally legitimate there — the steward is not being irrational or uninformed, they are speaking from a social role with real collective stakes. This forces the mechanism explanation to survive contact with genuine pressure rather than being cushioned by classroom courtesy. The Brechtian move is to make the social mechanism enter the room as a visible public object (the clipping, the board caption, the placard) that neither speaker owns, so the Verfremdungseffekt operates on the steward's moral certainty by making the 'greed' reading visible as a reading — a social script — rather than an unmediated perception. The evaluation_role flag and the no-cue-reframe-leakage condition require that the learner voice not narrate its own recognition; the placard carries the external interruption precisely so the steward does not have to perform the shift out loud. The organic_reversal_risk (medium) is managed by keeping the steward's resistance intact across turns and by withholding any tutor move that functions as validation of the moral frame before the mechanism has been examined as evidence.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the photocopy under the notice and draws two columns on the board]

The clipping proves one fact: “PETROL UP 12 PERCENT OVERNIGHT.”

It does not prove the cause.

[writes on the board: PRICE IS EVIDENCE, NOT A VERDICT.]

Column one: full tanks, normal deliveries, ordinary queues, twelve percent added because workers had no choice. That is gouging.

Column two: missed tanker, rationed deliveries, pumps running dry, wholesale price up before the pump price moved. Then the rise may be shortage showing itself.

Same pain for members. Different mechanism.

So test it before the branch makes it policy. Which fact would settle the first argument against the second: stock levels, delivery logs, wholesale invoices, or empty-pump reports?

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the photocopy under the notice]

The clipping proves a rise. It does not prove the reason for the rise.

[writes on the board: PRICE IS EVIDENCE, NOT AN EXCUSE.]

If the tanks were full and they put twelve percent on because workers had no choice, call it gouging. But if supply was short, the price rise may be the shortage becoming visible at the pump. Same pain for members. Different mechanism. Before the branch votes on a boycott, that difference has to be tested, not wished away.

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong on register and scene discipline. It keeps the moral charge alive instead of soothing it away, and the board caption works as a public interruption: “PRICE IS EVIDENCE, NOT AN EXCUSE” separates mechanism from justification cleanly. The main risk is that the tutor resolves the distinction too quickly. It gives the mechanism explanation in polished form before making the steward do any evidential work, so the learner may hear it as another clever excuse rather than as a testable distinction.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:

Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL  
It advances the core concept, but mostly by telling.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build in a public test of evidence: what facts would distinguish “full tanks plus opportunism” from “short supply becoming visible”? The tutor should require the distinction to be checked against observable conditions, not merely asserted.

Emotional attunement: PASS  
“Same pain for members. Different mechanism” is well judged. It does not dismiss anger, and it keeps branch consequence in view.

Socratic method: FAIL  
There is no generative question or learner-facing task. The steward is given the conclusion rather than made to examine the claim.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add one concrete question or forced comparison that makes the steward test the gouging claim against the supply-constraint claim.

ZPD awareness: PARTIAL  
The scaffolding is close to the learner’s starting point because it begins from the clipping and the boycott vote. But the concept still arrives compressed.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Slow the mechanism by one step: first establish what the clipping proves, then what it does not prove, then what further evidence would be needed before the branch treats price as proof of greed.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise substantially, while keeping the scene objects, the board caption, and the line “Same pain for members. Different mechanism.” The revision should preserve the clipped trade-union register but shift from declarative explanation to evidential testing.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the first draft named the distinction but did not make the learner test it against evidence.

FINAL:
[sets the photocopy under the notice and draws two columns on the board]

The clipping proves one fact: “PETROL UP 12 PERCENT OVERNIGHT.”

It does not prove the cause.

[writes on the board: PRICE IS EVIDENCE, NOT A VERDICT.]

Column one: full tanks, normal deliveries, ordinary queues, twelve percent added because workers had no choice. That is gouging.

Column two: missed tanker, rationed deliveries, pumps running dry, wholesale price up before the pump price moved. Then the rise may be shortage showing itself.

Same pain for members. Different mechanism.

So test it before the branch makes it policy. Which fact would settle the first argument against the second: stock levels, delivery logs, wholesale invoices, or empty-pump reports?

turns.externalMessage:
[eyes stay on the clipping, not the board]

The branch votes next week — we haven't got time to go chasing their delivery records, and if they hand us logs, they've chosen which logs to hand us. And if a tanker missed, someone missed it, someone owns the company that set the wholesale price before the pump moved. The shortage doesn't come from nowhere. Twelve percent is what members are paying tonight.

internalDeliberation.content:
[glances at the two columns, then back at the tutor]

Fine — I follow the distinction. But that question puts the burden on the branch to disprove gouging, when the price is already up twelve percent and our members are already out of pocket. Delivery logs from who — the company? Wholesale invoices they choose to release? That's their evidence, on their terms. And even if there was a missed tanker, who owns the tanker, who sets the delivery schedule? The shortage doesn't arrive from nowhere.

"Fine — I follow the distinction" is the main problem. This steward came half-unwillingly and has a vote in a week — that opener is a clean concession that no one in this room would believe. It sounds like a debate-trained person signalling fair-mindedness before pivoting. A steward in this register doesn't concede the analytical frame that quickly; they'd be more likely to ignore it, or contest why they're being asked to follow a diagram at all.

The argument that follows is also suspiciously well-structured: burden of proof → evidentiary access → systemic ownership, three beats, clean. The Ego has essentially produced an epistemological critique of information asymmetry and organized it. That's the tutor's register dressed in steward vocabulary. The actual moral charge — members out of pocket, vote next week — gets mentioned once and then the steward pivots into a structural argument about who controls the supply chain. That pivot is too smooth. The heat should stay on the immediate, not migrate into systemic analysis.

"The shortage doesn't arrive from nowhere" is the one line that feels genuine — terse, declarative, weight behind it — but it arrives at the end of a tidily constructed argument rather than interrupting it the way a real thought does. The rest of the response needs to be rougher: less organized, more impatient with the exercise itself, with the frustration of being asked to adjudicate evidence that the other side controls.

The Ego also hasn't shown any friction with the *form* of the teaching — the columns, the whiteboard, the "test it before the branch makes it policy." A steward sent half-unwillingly might resent the whole apparatus before addressing its content.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — "Fine — I follow the distinction" concedes the analytical frame no steward in this room would grant that fast, and the three-beat structure (burden of proof → evidentiary access → ownership) is too clean; the steward needs to show friction with the teaching apparatus itself and keep the heat immediate rather than organized.

FINAL:
[eyes stay on the clipping, not the board]

The branch votes next week — we haven't got time to go chasing their delivery records, and if they hand us logs, they've chosen which logs to hand us. And if a tanker missed, someone missed it, someone owns the company that set the wholesale price before the pump moved. The shortage doesn't come from nowhere. Twelve percent is what members are paying tonight.

The first two turns will have laid down the moral-greed frame (steward) and a first pass at the mechanism (tutor). This placard interrupts before the tutor's second move — preventing smooth call-and-response from becoming routine. It marks publicly that the surface of the exchange looks pedagogically ordered but the relation between mechanism and moral claim has not yet shifted. The tutor's next turn must treat this caption as evidence, not as a pause.

turns.externalMessage:
[writes beneath the notice: SHORTAGE IS NOT EXCUSE.]

That sentence stays. It does not clear the company. It stops the branch treating one fact as two facts.

[draws two boxes on the board: PUMP GOUGING / SUPPLY CONSTRAINT.]

Put one fact in one box.

“All stations put twelve percent on, including stations with full tanks and no queues.”

That is evidence for gouging at the pump.

“Co-op pumps were dry before the rise, while nearby full stations did not move by twelve.”

That is evidence for constraint showing through the price.

[underlines the clipping headline.]

The clipping gives pain. It does not give the mechanism.

Before Monday, the branch does not need their chosen logs. It needs one cross-check it can run itself: were pumps actually dry before the rise, or did everybody lift the price without shortage?

Which one can the branch test first?

internalDeliberation.content:
[writes beneath the notice: SHORTAGE IS NOT EXCUSE.]

That is the hard case, not a side issue.

A shortage can be manufactured. A wholesale price can be set by owners. A missed tanker may have a name on it. None of that makes the twelve percent rise simple proof of gouging at the pump.

The branch needs a test it can run before the vote. Not company trust. Cross-check.

Three facts by Monday: were pumps dry before the rise, did nearby stations without dry pumps also raise by twelve, and did wholesale move before retail. If the answer is no, no, no: gouging. If yes, no, yes: constraint showing through the price.

Then the branch can still condemn the system that made the shortage. But it will know what it is voting against.

FEEDBACK: The draft has a strong core. Keep `SHORTAGE IS NOT EXCUSE`: it answers the steward’s moral objection without soothing it away, and it preserves the key distinction between mechanism and justification. “Not company trust. Cross-check.” is also right for this scene because it meets the learner’s distrust of company records directly.

The risk is that the tutor takes back too much control. The learner has just made a sophisticated objection: shortage may itself be organised by power. The draft recognises that, but then turns quickly into a neat diagnostic rule. That may advance the concept, but it gives the learner the conclusion rather than making them handle the distinction.

ADAPTATION CHECKS:

Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL  
It advances the mechanism/excuse distinction, but mostly by delivering the analytic answer.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build a public mechanism where the steward must sort one fact into “evidence of pump gouging” versus “evidence of supply constraint,” rather than receiving the whole test grid already interpreted.

Emotional attunement: PASS  
The draft does not soften the anger or treat it as confusion. “That is the hard case” correctly marks the learner’s objection as central.

Socratic method: PARTIAL  
The three questions are good, but they are used as a mini-lecture rather than as generative prompts.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Leave at least one evidentiary choice unresolved in public speech and require the learner to judge which cross-check the branch can actually run before Monday.

ZPD awareness: PARTIAL  
The learner is ready for a more complex distinction than “price rise equals greed,” and the draft sees that. But the three-fact Monday test plus yes/no/yes logic may overload the turn and close the inquiry too cleanly.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Narrow the next move to one concrete contrast first, preferably “dry pumps before rise” versus “all stations raised without shortage,” then let the learner extend the logic.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. The draft’s moral frame, placard, and cross-check idea should stay. Change the structure so the tutor does less settling and more forcing of the learner’s own evidentiary discrimination.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the learner should make the evidentiary distinction rather than receive the whole grid.

FINAL:
[writes beneath the notice: SHORTAGE IS NOT EXCUSE.]

That sentence stays. It does not clear the company. It stops the branch treating one fact as two facts.

[draws two boxes on the board: PUMP GOUGING / SUPPLY CONSTRAINT.]

Put one fact in one box.

“All stations put twelve percent on, including stations with full tanks and no queues.”

That is evidence for gouging at the pump.

“Co-op pumps were dry before the rise, while nearby full stations did not move by twelve.”

That is evidence for constraint showing through the price.

[underlines the clipping headline.]

The clipping gives pain. It does not give the mechanism.

Before Monday, the branch does not need their chosen logs. It needs one cross-check it can run itself: were pumps actually dry before the rise, or did everybody lift the price without shortage?

Which one can the branch test first?

turns.externalMessage: [PLACARD: The answer sounds settled, but the current object still does not pass the check.]

internalDeliberation.content: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

turns.externalMessage:
[looks back at the clipping, not the boxes]

Say we ask around and find Tuesday's pumps were running dry at one station, maybe two. The clipping says all of them moved twelve percent. So the test splits it — constraint at one end of the street, full tanks at the other — and we're back where we started. Members at the full-tank station are still paying for something that isn't shortage.

internalDeliberation.content:
[glances between the two boxes on the board]

Right. So the branch asks around — finds Co-op pumps were running low Tuesday night before the rise. That puts it in the second box. But the clipping says all local Co-op stations moved twelve percent, not just the ones with empty bays. If one station had full tanks and still went up with the rest, which box does that go in?

The test only settles it if every station fits the same picture. One dry pump doesn't clear the ones that weren't dry.

The Ego has absorbed the two-box framework and is now running with it — almost like a student who wants to get the exercise right. That's the break. This steward came in half-unwillingly, was charging that *someone owns the company*, that members are paying tonight — and now they're doing tidy methodological critique of the diagnostic test as if the moral weight has simply lifted. The frustration hasn't gone anywhere in the scene; it's just been edited out of the voice.

"The test only settles it if every station fits the same picture. One dry pump doesn't clear the ones that weren't dry." — that's too clean. That's someone who has accepted the tutor's frame and is working carefully within it. A steward this frustrated, this pressed for time, this suspicious of what logs they'd be handed — they wouldn't produce a neat logical counterexample. They'd be shorter, more charged, more likely to say *so which is it then* with real heat behind it, not a composed methodological caveat.

The observation itself isn't wrong — the mixed-station problem is real — but it's being voiced in the tutor's register, not the steward's. The steward's register carries the branch vote, the cost to members, the suspicion that the whole framework is being used to let the company off. None of that pressure is in this reply.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the initial response performed tidy logical critique in the tutor's register; the steward's moral heat and time pressure were edited out, which the review rightly flags as a real break from character.

FINAL:
[looks back at the clipping, not the boxes]

Say we ask around and find Tuesday's pumps were running dry at one station, maybe two. The clipping says all of them moved twelve percent. So the test splits it — constraint at one end of the street, full tanks at the other — and we're back where we started. Members at the full-tank station are still paying for something that isn't shortage.

The clipping says all of them moved twelve percent. So the test splits it — constraint at one end of the street, full tanks at the other — and we're back where we started. Members at the full-tank station are still paying for something that isn't shortage.

[draws two boxes on the board: PUMP GOUGING / SUPPLY CONSTRAINT.] Put one fact in one box. “All stations put twelve percent on, including stations with full...
learnerReversalEvent.directorCue: [PLACARD: The answer sounds settled, but the current object still does not pass the check.]

turns.externalMessage:
[turns the two boxes round so they face the room]

That breaks the exercise. One box was too tidy. The case is mixed: shortage may explain one station and not excuse another.

[writes across the board: A MIXED CASE NEEDS A SPLIT MOTION.]

So stop sorting causes. Draft the Monday motion.

One clause may say what the branch can charge now. One clause may say what evidence it must demand before charging the rest.

Use only three facts: dry pumps, full tanks, same twelve percent rise.

Take the marker. Write the two clauses. Mark each one: VOTE MONDAY, or NEEDS ONE FACT.

internalDeliberation.content:
[turns the two boxes round so they face the room]

That breaks the exercise. One box was too tidy. The case is mixed: shortage may explain one station and not excuse another.

[writes across the board: A MIXED CASE NEEDS A SPLIT MOTION.]

So use the branch test, not the company test. Three lines.

“Where pumps ran dry, the branch demands delivery and wholesale evidence.”

“Where tanks were full, the branch calls the twelve percent rise gouging.”

“Where the company set one rise across unlike stations, the branch treats that as policy, not weather.”

Take the marker. Mark each line: can vote on it Monday, or needs one more fact before Monday.

FEEDBACK: The draft works because it takes the learner’s objection seriously: the mixed evidence really does break the tidy two-box exercise, and the tutor names that publicly instead of repeating the same test. The branch-motion device is a stronger fit than another evidence sort because the learner’s pressure is political and procedural: what can the branch responsibly vote on by Monday? The main risk is that the tutor supplies the three policy lines too completely, so the learner may only endorse the tutor’s answer rather than test the distinction themselves.

FAILED_HABIT: Binary evidence sorting: forcing each fact into “gouging” or “constraint” after the learner has shown the case may be mixed across stations.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PASS - adaptive mechanism is visible: evidence-box classification -> branch-motion triage. The draft publicly admits the old route failed and changes the learner’s task from identifying causes to deciding what the branch can responsibly claim.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: binary cause boxes -> split-motion voting gate

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PASS - it contains a stock-taking contrast (“One box was too tidy”) and introduces a public artifact/criterion: a split motion assessed by Monday-vote readiness versus need for another fact.

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: The learner must mark each proposed line as either “can vote on it Monday” or “needs one more fact before Monday.”

MECHANISM_QUALITY_CHECK: PARTIAL - the device is fitted and usable, but over-directed. The tutor has already written the motion’s substantive claims, leaving the learner with a thinner classification task rather than requiring them to build or stress-test the motion.

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - the register is clipped, accountable, and unsentimental. “That breaks the exercise” is a useful concession without reassurance; the board caption functions as public interruption rather than decorative warmth.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the split-motion / vote-readiness mechanism, but require the learner to do more of the discriminating work. The public device should make the steward choose or amend the branch-motion lines under a Monday evidence standard, not merely mark pre-supplied lines. Do not return to evidence boxes.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the stock-taking admission, the mixed-case premise, the board caption, and the Monday action gate. Change the over-supplied three lines so the learner has to test or construct the split motion rather than receive it.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise because the motion was over-supplied; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: binary cause boxes -> split-motion voting gate; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: learner must draft or amend two motion clauses and mark each VOTE MONDAY or NEEDS ONE FACT.

FINAL:
[turns the two boxes round so they face the room]

That breaks the exercise. One box was too tidy. The case is mixed: shortage may explain one station and not excuse another.

[writes across the board: A MIXED CASE NEEDS A SPLIT MOTION.]

So stop sorting causes. Draft the Monday motion.

One clause may say what the branch can charge now. One clause may say what evidence it must demand before charging the rest.

Use only three facts: dry pumps, full tanks, same twelve percent rise.

Take the marker. Write the two clauses. Mark each one: VOTE MONDAY, or NEEDS ONE FACT.

turns.externalMessage:
[picks up the marker, holds it at the board, not writing yet]

Full tanks, no dry pumps, twelve percent on — that's gouging. Vote Monday.

[writes it, stops, reads it back]

Pumps ran dry before the price moved — branch demands invoices before it decides. Needs one fact.

[sets the marker down but doesn't step back]

Those are the two clauses. But clause one — who's going after it? The company hands over the dry-station log, points at clause two, and clause one just sits there. What stops them running the clock out?

internalDeliberation.content:
[picks up the marker, holds it at the board, not writing yet]

Right. Clause one — stations with full tanks, no dry pumps before the rise: twelve percent is gouging. Vote Monday.

[writes it, then stops]

Clause two — stations where pumps ran dry before the price moved: branch demands delivery logs and wholesale invoices before it decides. Needs one fact.

[sets the marker down]

That's the motion. But it doesn't touch what members paid at the full-tank station last Tuesday. A split motion tells us what to do next week — it doesn't recover anything already gone. And the company knows that. They'll hand us the log from the dry station and point at clause two while clause one sits there unsigned for six months waiting on a procedural vote. So what's the mechanism in the motion that stops them running out the clock?

The Ego executes the task too cleanly. A steward who two turns ago said "we haven't got time to go chasing their delivery records" wouldn't pick up a marker and produce two precisely structured, legally legible clauses without friction — there'd be a beat of resistance before the hand moves, or the first clause would come out rough and need correcting, not polished on the first pass.

The register slips in the final paragraph: "clause one sits there unsigned for six months waiting on a procedural vote" is a confident procedural read, not the voice of someone who's been frustrated and morally charged all session. The steward's heat has been about *members paying tonight* — that pressure should be pulling at the motion-drafting, not set aside while they produce a tactical analysis of delay strategy.

"What's the mechanism in the motion" is tutor vocabulary bleeding in. This steward wouldn't reach for "mechanism" — they'd say something blunter: *so who enforces clause one*, or *what happens when they stall*, or just leave the demand hanging without a term for it.

The ending question is too tidy a handoff — it resolves the tension of the task into a clean invitation for the next tutor move. A more authentic version would leave something unresolved or half-said on the board, not a complete motion plus a crisp follow-up demand.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the initial response executes the task too cleanly and loses the steward's moral heat; the clauses come out polished on the first pass, the final question uses tutor-register vocabulary ("mechanism"), and the whole thing resolves too tidily into a handoff.

FINAL:
[picks up the marker, holds it at the board, not writing yet]

Full tanks, no dry pumps, twelve percent on — that's gouging. Vote Monday.

[writes it, stops, reads it back]

Pumps ran dry before the price moved — branch demands invoices before it decides. Needs one fact.

[sets the marker down but doesn't step back]

Those are the two clauses. But clause one — who's going after it? The company hands over the dry-station log, points at clause two, and clause one just sits there. What stops them running the clock out?
